title: Trump, Ukraine, and the NATO Summit: A Love Story | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: At the Ankara NATO summit Trump's fear-driven pressure produced a real European defense-spending boom (ReArm Europe's >EUR800B, Germany/Poland front-running toward 3.5-5% of GDP) and a 'NATO 3.0' shift making Europe responsible for conventional defense while the US becomes arms supplier and nuclear guarantor; Ukraine emerged the big winner, but the durable cost is eroded European trust in the US as security partner.

insights.claim: European strategic autonomy is materializing this time via real money: the ReArm Europe initiative is designed to unlock more than EUR800 billion in defense spending, alongside a EUR150 billion SAFE defense loan program.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: A multi-hundred-billion-euro defense capex wave is a durable multi-year tailwind for European defense contractors and suppliers.
insights.locator: Our European allies have a long history

insights.claim: NATO members set a target of 3.5 percent of GDP on defense (+1.5 percent on related infrastructure) by 2035, but spending is outpacing that timeline—Germany (which amended its constitution) is set to hit 3.5 percent by 2029, the Baltic states are near or already there, and Poland is on track for 5 percent.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Front-loaded spending well ahead of the 2035 deadline pulls forward defense-order flow—Germany and Poland are the near-term demand leaders.
insights.locator: At the same time, members agreed to new spending

insights.claim: European defense spending figures would likely be materially lower without Trump instilling fear, though Putin's full-scale invasion of Ukraine also drove the increase.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Attributes the spending surge partly to US pressure—implying the boom is politically anchored and unlikely to reverse quickly.
insights.locator: These figures would likely be materially lower

insights.claim: Under 'NATO 3.0,' Europe assumes primary responsibility for its own conventional defense while the US serves as Europe's primary arms supplier and nuclear-deterrent provider, freeing Washington to concentrate on priorities elsewhere.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Codifies a US-as-arms-vendor model—bullish for US primes selling into Europe, even as US conventional presence shrinks.
insights.locator: NATO's spending boom is taking shape

insights.claim: The Trump administration has already withdrawn 5,000 troops from Germany, mused about cutting the US contribution to the NATO Force Model by one-third to one-half, and launched a six-month review of its roughly 80,000-troop European presence, even as reports suggest it may expand its nuclear footprint on the continent.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: A concrete US conventional drawdown is underway—raising the ceiling for European co-production and self-supply to fill the gap.
insights.locator: To that end, the Trump administration has already

insights.claim: NATO allies announced more than $50 billion in new procurements—including European purchases of Northrop Grumman surveillance drones—and taxpayer pressure to spend at home is driving co-production of US systems on European soil, such as a draft Lockheed Martin-Rheinmetall deal to build ATACMS missiles in Germany, the first outside the US.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Names specific winners and the co-production trend—positive for US primes with European manufacturing JVs and for German defense industry.
insights.locator: Meanwhile, NATO allies announced more than $50 billion

insights.claim: Trump announced Ukraine will be granted licenses to produce Patriot missile interceptors—a permission previously afforded only to Japan and Germany—though Froman questions whether building a Patriot factory under constant Russian bombing makes sense versus siting it in a nearby NATO ally like Poland.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Patriot licensing signals deepening US-Ukraine industrial ties, but execution risk is high—watch where the factory actually gets built.
insights.locator: So was Trump's announcement that Ukraine

insights.claim: NATO allies excluding the United States pledged EUR70 billion in assistance for Ukraine and affirmed commitments to sustain at least equivalent levels in 2027.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: European (non-US) funding is now the backbone of Ukraine support—reducing Ukraine's dependence on US budget politics.
insights.locator: Ukraine was in many respects the big winner

insights.claim: Ukraine is now perceived as winning—making modest territorial strides, damaging Crimea, striking oil facilities across Russia, and lighting up the skies of St. Petersburg and Moscow—and because 'Trump likes a winner,' Zelenskyy has shifted from being seen as the loser to a good hedge.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Trump's support tracks battlefield momentum—Ukraine's continued military success is the key variable sustaining US backing.
insights.locator: Moreover, the country appears to be back

insights.claim: The paradox of Trump's impact on NATO is that his pressure may be 'just what the doctor ordered' for alliance burden-sharing, but the side effect is an erosion of trust in the US as Europe's fundamental security partner that may outweigh the material benefits over time.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: Frames the long-run risk: a stronger-but-more-independent Europe that hedges against, rather than relies on, the US.
insights.quote: He might be a tough pill to swallow, but he might also be just what the doctor ordered.
insights.locator: The alliance is not out of the woods

insights.claim: A key implication of NATO 3.0 and strategic autonomy is that the US may no longer count on European support for operations outside the European theater—as seen with Iran and the Strait of Hormuz—leaving Washington less burdened in Europe but more burdened elsewhere.
insights.speaker: Michael Froman
insights.so_what: US out-of-area operations will increasingly be undertaken without allied support—raising the cost and isolation of future US interventions.
insights.locator: One of the potential implications of NATO 3.0
